critic_raw: The use of blocked random evaluation windows in a time-series forecasting study raises a standard concern: random window selection can break temporal ordering and introduce future information into the evaluation. In time-series forecasting, chronological back-testing is the standard approach because it respects the causal structure — the model should only use past data to predict the future. Blocked random windows may inadvertently include windows where the evaluation period precedes some training data, which means the model may have trained on future data relative to those evaluation points. This is the standard objection to non-chronological splits in time-series work.
defender_raw: The team empirically verified before selecting the evaluation design that blocked random windows and rolling-origin evaluation produce equivalent results for this specific task. The pre-analysis on six months of historical data showed that residual autocorrelation drops to near zero beyond two hours after conditioning on temperature, weekday, holiday status, and recent lagged load. For a 15-minute-ahead forecast with nightly retraining, the process behaves close to i.i.d. at the evaluation horizon. The team also compared model rankings under rolling-origin versus blocked random windows across four historical periods and found less than 1% difference in MAPE with no model-order reversals. The evaluation plan explicitly pre-specified blocked random windows based on these empirical checks. The claim is explicitly limited to this short-horizon retrained forecasting task — not to seasonal drift or week-ahead forecasting.
adjudication_raw: The critic raises the standard objection to non-chronological time-series evaluation. The defender demonstrates that this objection was anticipated and empirically tested: residual dependence at the 15-minute horizon after conditioning is near zero, and model rankings are equivalent under both evaluation methods across four historical periods. The use of non-chronological splits is justified here precisely because the empirical evidence shows the process behaves near-i.i.d. at the chosen horizon for a nightly-retrained model. Defense wins.
all_issues_raised: non-chronological split concern for time-series — resolved by empirical pre-analysis showing near-zero residual dependence at 15-minute horizon
potential future-data contamination — resolved by blocked window design that prevents within-window contamination
